title: Double J Placement Methods Comparative Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Guide Wire Placement (Difficult)
content: Guidewire placement is not usually a problem, but it can sometimes be challenging; especially when dealing with impacted stones, strictures, cancers, and tortuous ureters. First, try a different wire. We typically start with a hydrophilic stiff shaft straight tip but recommend an angled tip wire under challenging situations. The stiffer shaft wire helps straighten tortuous ureters, and it seems to be easier to advance with less bowing. In some cases, we may pass two wires to help straighten an unusually tortuous ureter. This can be quickly done with a dual lumen ureteral catheter.

title: Double J Placement Methods Comparative Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Keep the Guide Wire Moist
content: Start at the most distal end of the guidewire and wipe just the tip with a wet sponge from proximal to distal. This avoids having the guidewire accidentally perforate the sponge and getting caught. Then wipe the rest of the guidewire from distal to proximal to prevent accidental extraction or movement of the guidewire. We keep a wet sponge handy on the patient’s knee and another on the side of the water basin to help keep these hydrophilic supplies moist and wet whenever they are used or manipulated.

title: Percutaneous Coronary Intervention -- Technique or Treatment -- Angiography
content: After preparing the access site for approach, usually the groin for the femoral artery approach, an introducer needle is inserted in the artery. A guidewire is passed, and the needle is withdrawn. Once the access is gained, a "sheath introducer" is inserted over the guidewire, which helps to keep the artery open. A sheath is then inserted through the sheath introducer. The sheaths are flexible hollow tubes used to introduce different catheters. Different types of sheaths are used to access different locations like right and left coronary arteries and left ventricle. With X-ray fluoroscopy, contrast material is introduced in the coronary artery to delineate its anatomy. Stenosis or occlusion of the coronary artery is then visualized, and severity is estimated through pictures at different angles. If stenosis or occlusion is located, the cardiologist then introduces a guidewire through the catheter and positions the tip of the wire distal to the stenosis in the artery. This guidewire is then used to introduce the balloon or stent catheter over it for angioplasty or stent placement, respectively. For stent placement, the catheter has the stent positioned over the balloon, and once in the right location, the balloon can be expanded, which stretches the stent open over the balloon. The catheter can then be withdrawn. Images are taken to confirm the proper location of the stent and resolution of stenosis.

title: Biliary Stenting -- Treatment / Management -- Technique of Stent Insertion
content: Materials required for plastic stent insertion include a radiopaque guidewire, stent insertion system, and dilators. The guidewire typically has a stiffer shaft and a hydrophilic tip to facilitate the passage of strictures. Guidewire systems often feature a locking mechanism to prevent slippage during exchange procedures. Some systems include an intraductal exchange feature, which allows the catheter to be removed while leaving the guidewire in the biliary tract, enabling the insertion of multiple plastic stents. The stent insertion system consists of a plastic guiding catheter (with radiopaque markers), the stent, and a pusher tube. A guiding catheter is not necessary for 7 Fr stents. content: An ultrasound unit, a fluoroscopic imaging system (such as a C-arm), lead shielding, sterile drapes, heparinized saline, and heparin is required for both types of approaches. An ACT machine should be available to ensure that the ACT after heparinization is greater than 250 seconds. The transfemoral approach requires the following equipment: micropuncture kit for percutaneous access of the common femoral artery, 0.035 inch and 0.014-inch wires, 6 French short and long sheaths, angled catheters, angioplasty balloons, embolic protection device or flow reversal system. The transcarotid approach requires the following: a surgical tray, vascular clamps to perform surgical exposure of the common carotid artery at the base of the neck and allow for direct puncture of the artery using a micropuncture kit, additional wires, sheaths, and balloons may be used as described above. a flow reversal system for neuroprotection against potential embolization.

title: Percutaneous Transluminal Coronary Angioplasty -- Technique or Treatment
content: The diagnostic catheter is then manipulated into the ostium of the left main coronary artery or right coronary artery. Contrast dye is injected, and cineangiography images are obtained in multiple views of both arteries. PTCA can be performed if severe stenosis exists in 1 of the arteries. The diagnostic catheter is removed and exchanged for a similar guide catheter. Guide catheters have a larger luminal diameter to pass wires and balloons during angioplasty easily. After the guide catheter is placed in the ostium of the respective artery, a PTCA guidewire is advanced through the catheter and across the stenosis. Once the PTCA guidewire is passed across the stenosis, it is left in place until the end of the procedure. A balloon wire can be placed over the PTCA guidewire and advanced until the balloon is directly over the stenosis. The cardiologist controls the direction and movement of the PTCA guidewire and balloon wire by twisting the part of the guidewire that sits outside the patient.

title: Double J Placement Methods Comparative Analysis -- Introduction -- Features of an Optimal Double J Stent Placement Technique: Basic Principles
content: The dangler/safety thread needs to be removed in a manner that will not move the stent out of position. The dangler thread can easily be cut and removed at the end of the case as long as the pusher is still in place and the guidewire is always at least partially inside the distal end of the double J stent. The partially inserted guidewire and pusher together stabilize the distal end of the stent and keeps everything properly aligned with only gentle traction on the dangler thread. Without the thread, there is no way to keep the stent from moving during manipulation or to extract it without losing the guidewire and possibly ureteral access as well. Once the thread is removed, the guidewire can be quickly and easily withdrawn without dislodging the stent since the pusher will prevent distal stent migration and inadvertent extraction.

content: If stenting is planned, after balloon dilation, the catheter is withdrawn, and a stent of appropriate size and diameter is advanced over the guidewire. Once the stent is in the desired artery, contrast material is injected, and the stent is positioned and deployed across the lesion, followed by another angiography. Intravascular imaging tools like Intravascular ultrasonography or Optic coherence tomography are used for assessment of the adequacy of stent deployment, including stent expansion and apposition. If post stent deployment angiograms are deemed adequate, the guidewire is withdrawn. Hemostasis is achieved by direct manual compression or application of a compression band to the wrist (radial) or vascular closure devices (femoral).

title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 926
content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass a range of catheter‑based interventions designed to treat coronary artery disease, most notably percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) and coronary thrombolysis. Both ptca and coronary thrombolysis are classified as forms of interventional cardiac catheterization, indicating that they are performed through vascular access to the coronary circulation. Ptca is a mechanical technique used to dilate narrowed coronary segments and is a specific subcategory within the broader Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures. Coronary thrombolysis, which employs pharmacologic agents to dissolve thrombotic occlusions, is also a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and serves the purpose of removal of coronary artery obstruction. A recognized clinical sequela of ptca is the occurrence of recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, underscoring the importance of post‑procedure monitoring and possible additional therapeutic measures. Together, these interventional cardiac catheterization procedures provide complementary strategies for restoring coronary patency and optimizing patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 760
content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass several interventional techniques used to restore patency of coronary vessels. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) is classified as a specific type of both Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and interventional cardiac catheterization, reflecting its role as a catheter‑based method to dilate stenotic coronary arteries. Coronary thrombolysis is likewise a component of Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and is considered a form of interventional cardiac catheterization; it serves as the parent concept for the broader procedure described as removal of coronary artery obstruction. The removal of coronary artery obstruction, which is linked to the Arabic term “رأف الوعاء,” is therefore understood as a specific application of coronary thrombolysis aimed at clearing occlusive material from the coronary lumen. Ptca is also associated with the term “رأف الوعاء” through a reference relationship, indicating that both ptca and removal of obstruction share the underlying goal of vessel reperfusion. Clinically, recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty can occur, highlighting the need for careful follow‑up and possible adjunctive therapies such as coronary thrombolysis or repeat obstruction removal. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the hierarchy and functional connections among catheter‑based coronary interventions, their therapeutic categories, and the potential post‑procedural complications that guide patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
content: Contrast agents, classified under the category “indikátory a reagencie,” play a central role in a wide range of diagnostic imaging and interventional radiology procedures. In computed tomography of the thorax, the use of contrast material distinguishes two distinct protocols: a contrast‑enhanced CT thorax, which explicitly utilizes contrast agents to improve vascular and tissue delineation, and a non‑contrast CT thorax, which deliberately excludes contrast substances. Beyond CT, contrast agents are routinely employed in fluoroscopic angiography of the femoral, intracranial, lower‑limb, and upper‑limb arteries, where they enhance visualization of arterial anatomy and facilitate accurate thrombolytic therapy. Fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis procedures, whether targeting intracranial vessels or other vascular territories, also rely on contrast media to confirm catheter placement and monitor the distribution of thrombolytic agents. Similarly, percutaneous thrombolysis of the axillary vein and of central venous catheters under fluoroscopic guidance incorporates contrast substances to ensure precise navigation and effective treatment. Collectively, these applications underscore the essential function of contrast agents in improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes across multiple vascular imaging and interventional contexts.

Fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis of an intracranial vessel with contrast and fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis with contrast similarly rely on contrast substances to enhance imaging and guide treatment. Percutaneous thrombolysis of the axillary vein using fluoroscopic guidance with contrast and percutaneous thrombolysis of a central venous catheter using fluoroscopic guidance with contrast also depend on contrast agents for accurate catheter placement, visualization of the vein or catheter, and assessment of thrombolytic effect. title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 927
content: Other vascular catheterization — not involving the heart — covers a range of arterial and venous catheter‑based techniques that are used for hemodynamic monitoring, drug delivery, and vessel embolization. Arterial cannulation with cardiac‑output monitoring, percutaneous insertion of catheters into the left radial artery for monitoring, and percutaneous insertion of catheters into the right femoral artery for monitoring are all classified as specific forms of this non‑cardiac vascular catheterization. Open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization that administers thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, as well as percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization that delivers the same agents by pulse‑spray technique or by continuous infusion, are also primary examples of other vascular catheterization. Transcatheter embolization of a blood vessel is another procedure that falls under this category. Each of these catheterization methods serves as a conduit for the injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substances, linking the procedural techniques directly to the delivery of drugs such as thrombolytics, chemotherapeutics, or other agents. Together, these interconnected procedures illustrate how non‑cardiac vascular catheterization provides essential pathways for monitoring circulatory parameters and administering targeted therapies in clinical practice.

content: Contrast agents (kontrastní látky) are classified under the broader category of indicators and reagents (indikátory a reagencie). They serve as essential substances in a wide range of imaging‑guided vascular interventions. In fluoroscopic angiography, contrast agents are employed together with thrombolytic therapy for the femoral artery, intracranial artery, lower‑limb artery, and upper‑limb artery, enabling visualization of the vascular lumen during clot dissolution. Fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis also relies on contrast agents, both for general intracranial vessel procedures and for non‑specific thrombolysis applications. Percutaneous thrombolysis procedures that use fluoroscopic guidance—such as those targeting the axillary vein or a central venous catheter—similarly depend on contrast agents to delineate the target vessel and monitor treatment progress. Computed tomography of the thorax illustrates a diagnostic workflow in which the examination may begin without contrast material and subsequently incorporate contrast material(s) for additional sections, highlighting the flexible use of contrast agents to enhance anatomical detail when needed. content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA), which is a specific coronary therapeutic procedure performed via interventional cardiac catheterization. PTCA is also described in Arabic as “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد,” reflecting its percutaneous nature. Interventional cardiac catheterization therefore includes both PTCA and coronary thrombolysis as its constituent techniques. Coronary thrombolysis is a method used for the removal of coronary artery obstruction and is classified as a subtype of interventional cardiac catheterization. The relationship between these modalities places PTCA and coronary thrombolysis under the broader category of coronary therapeutic services, while also recognizing coronary thrombolysis as a therapeutic approach aimed at clearing obstructed coronary vessels. content: Other vascular catheterization; not heart is a broad interventional category that includes any invasive placement of catheters in arteries or veins outside the heart for diagnostic, monitoring, or therapeutic purposes.
